It says that if you have more pigeons than pigeonholes, at least one pigeonhole has more than one pigeon. But here, I need two matching pairs. So maybe I need to consider the worst-case scenario where I pull out socks in a way that delays getting two matching pairs as much as possible. Let me think. For one pair, the worst case would be pulling out one sock of each color first. So with three colors, that's 3 socks, and the next sock would ensure a pair. So 3 + 1 = 4 socks to guarantee one pair. But here, we need two pairs. How does that work? Wait, two pairs could be of the same color or different colors. For example, two red and two blue, or four red socks (which would make two pairs). But the question is to guarantee at least two pairs regardless of how the socks are pulled out. So to find the minimum number needed to ensure that even in the worst case, there are two pairs. Let me consider the worst-case scenario. Let's try to maximize the number of socks pulled without getting two pairs. How can that happen? First, maybe pulling out as many single socks as possible. But since we need two pairs, perhaps the worst case is having as many single socks as possible plus one pair. Wait, but we need to avoid getting two pairs. So the maximum number of socks without having two pairs would be one pair plus all single socks of the other colors. But maybe I need to think differently. Alternatively, think of the maximum number of socks without two pairs. Let me think in terms of pairs and single socks. If you want to have as many socks as possible with only one pair, you can have one pair (2 socks) and then one sock of each other color. But since there are two other colors, that would be 2 + 1 + 1 = 4 socks. But if you have more colors? Wait, the problem has three colors. But if you take one more sock, say another red, you now have two pairs (two red pairs and maybe another pair if you have two of another color). Wait, no, if you have three red socks, that's one pair and one extra. So, still only one pair. Wait, no, three socks of the same color would give one pair and one single. So, to have two pairs, you need at least two different pairs. So maybe the worst case is having as many socks as possible with only one pair and the rest singles. If you add another sock, if it matches one of the single socks, you get a second pair. If it matches the existing pair, you get three of a color, which still only counts as one pair (since a pair is two). Wait, but three socks of one color would contain one pair, and the third is a single. So, to have two pairs, you need two different pairs. So the worst case is when you have one pair and as many singles as possible. But how many singles can you have?
